fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this text classification and semantic search toolkit that's been on the backlog. The core idea is developers drop in plain text and get back useful signals — categories, scores, ranked docs, that kind of thing. We had a similar pipeline in the old recommendation engine (you can probably grep around for how we handled the scoring edge cases there), so let's not reinvent the wheel too much.

Main things I care about: the tokenizer needs to be smart about what it keeps vs throws away depending on config, the Bayes classifier should handle the case where someone queries something totally foreign to the training data (users have been complaining the old one just confidently returns garbage instead of saying 'I don't know'), and the semantic index piece needs to stay usable even after someone reorganizes which documents belong to which bucket — without having to blow everything away and start over.

Also there's been a recurring complaint that when people index a document that's basically just filler words, the system silently accepts it and then behaves weirdly downstream. We want a clear signal when that happens.

One more thing — the serialization story needs to actually work end to end. Someone on the data team tried to save and reload an index last sprint and the rankings came back different. That's a blocker.

Let's make sure the output format is consistent and deterministic across all these features. cc @backend-platform

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. On the token counting side, the behavior around punctuation needs to stay very explicit. When include_punctuation is true, individual punctuation characters like '!', '.', and "'" are their own tokens and get counted independently. They still go through the same stopword removal logic as the word tokens. When include_punctuation=false, punctuation tokens are excluded entirely before counting. That’s the same general expectation for the tokenization normalization piece in Feature 1 too: when include_punctuation=true, punctuation characters are kept as tokens but stopwords are still removed; when false, both punctuation and stopwords are excluded. Also, the output here should be token=count pairs sorted lexically.

For the vector reporting bit, each vector should come back as two lines in the same format every time: 'vector_N_length=<len>' and then either 'vector_N_zero=yes' or 'vector_N_zero=no'. Vectors are indexed from 0. An empty vector with length 0 counts as a zero vector, so that should output 'vector_N_zero=yes', and a vector containing only 0 values should also output 'yes'.

On the decomposition output, please keep it dead simple and line-based. We want input_rows, input_columns, left_rows, left_columns, right_rows, right_columns, and singular_values, all using the '=' delimiter format, one per line. singular_values should be comma-separated values rounded to two decimal places.

For training behavior, if auto_categorize is false and someone tries to train with a category label we don’t know, the system should return exactly 'error=category_not_found\ncategory=<label>\n' and not modify state at all. If auto_categorize is true, then we create the category automatically and report the updated sorted category list and count.

Also confirming the low-confidence classification behavior since a few people asked the same thing in different ways: if there’s a confidence threshold configured and the best score for a query is below that threshold, the category should be 'none'. So for example, 'xyzzy=none'. Same deal in Feature 5.4: if the best classification score is below the configured threshold value, like -4.0, the output must be '<query>=none' rather than returning some weak category anyway. If the query meets or exceeds the threshold, it should output the matched category name normally.

And on the semantic index piece, yes, recategorizing documents is supposed to work in place without forcing a rebuild. The output should include 'needs_rebuild=no' to make that explicit, and after the recategorization happens, subsequent classifications should reflect the updated category assignments immediately.

trigger_keywords: what warning is produced when an indexed document has only stopwords
how does the system signal that a document produced no usable terms
what is the output format for an empty or stopword-only document warning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a document contains only stopwords or words too short to produce usable terms, the system emits a normalized warning in the format: 'warning=empty_document\ndocument=<original text>\n'. The original document text is preserved verbatim in the warning output.

trigger_keywords: what score format is used when a query has no usable evidence against a category
how is an infinite score represented in classification output
what happens when all training text for a category was stopwords and a query matches it
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a query produces a classification score that is mathematically infinite (e.g., because the matched category had no valid training evidence after stopword removal), the score is reported as the literal string 'infinity' rather than a numeric value, e.g., 'query_0_score=infinity'.

trigger_keywords: does the serialized index return the same rankings after restore
are search results consistent before and after saving and loading a semantic index
what guarantees does the serialization provide for ranked output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Serialization and restoration of the semantic index must preserve ranked search results exactly. The output format uses 'original_rank_N=<text>' for pre-serialization results and 'restored_rank_N=<text>' for post-restoration results, and both sequences must be identical.

trigger_keywords: how are categories sorted when listing them after training
what order do categories appear in when reported after adding a new one
is the category list alphabetically sorted in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Category lists in output are always sorted alphabetically (lexicographically). This applies to the 'initial_categories', 'final_categories', and 'categories' output fields. For example, adding 'Test' to ['Interesting', 'Uninteresting'] produces 'final_categories=Interesting,Test,Uninteresting'.

trigger_keywords: how does untraining affect classification predictions
what is the expected output format showing before and after untraining
does removing learned evidence change the predicted category for a query
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Untraining removes previously learned evidence from a category. The output reports the prediction before untraining as 'before=<Category>' and after as 'after=<Category>'. Note that category names in output are title-cased regardless of how they were originally provided (e.g., 'colors' becomes 'Colors' in output).

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for keyword extraction
how are extracted keywords and summary sentences formatted in output
what does the summary output look like when extracting from a document collection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Keyword extraction outputs 'keywords=<stem1>,<stem2>,<stem3>' with the top-ranked stemmed keywords comma-separated. Summary outputs 'summary=<sentence1> [...] <sentence2>' where representative sentences are separated by ' [...] ' and sentences may be truncated. Both are on separate lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: someone reorganizes which documents belong to which bucket — without having to blow everything away and start over
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6.5 (semantic recategorization): the SemanticIndex supports reassigning category labels to already-indexed documents in-place, confirmed by 'needs_rebuild=no' in output, without requiring the full document index to be reconstructed.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the system silently accepts it and then behaves weirdly downstream
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6.9 (document validation warning): when a document added to the semantic index yields no usable terms after tokenization (all stopwords or too-short words), the system must emit 'warning=empty_document\ndocument=<original text>\n' rather than silently indexing an empty vector.

context_pointers.vague_reference: someone on the data team tried to save and reload an index last sprint and the rankings came back different
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6.7 (serialized index consistency): the semantic index serialization/deserialization cycle must produce byte-for-byte identical ranked search results, reported as matching 'original_rank_N' and 'restored_rank_N' output lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have enough context on that specific detail from my side. You'll need to dig into the existing codebase or check with the engineering lead — there may be prior decisions baked in that aren't documented in this ticket. Try grepping for related logic or check the test cases for the relevant feature.

feature1_token_frequency.json.description: Counts meaningful terms in text after configurable punctuation filtering, stopword removal, and optional stemming.

input.text: here are some good words of test's. I hope you love them!

cases.expected_output:
!=1
'=1
.=1
good=1
hope=1
love=1
test=1
them=1
word=1

input.text: here are some good words of test's. I hope you love them!

cases.expected_output:
good=1
hope=1
love=1
test=1
them=1
word=1

input.text: here are some good words of test's. I hope you love them!

cases.expected_output:
good=1
hope=1
love=1
tests=1
them=1
words=1

feature2_zero_vector_detection.json.description: Reports whether numeric vectors are zero vectors, including empty vectors and vectors containing only zero values.

cases.expected_output:
vector_0_length=0
vector_0_zero=yes
vector_1_length=1
vector_1_zero=yes
vector_2_length=10
vector_2_zero=yes

feature3_matrix_singular_decomposition.j.description: Decomposes a numeric matrix and reports the dimensions of decomposition outputs plus rounded singular values.

cases.expected_output:
input_rows=2
input_columns=2
left_rows=2
left_columns=2
right_rows=2
right_columns=2
singular_values=1.00,1.00

feature4_ordered_word_vocabulary.json.description: Maintains an ordered unique vocabulary where duplicate additions do not increase size and positions follow first insertion order.

cases.expected_output:
size_after_0=1
size_after_1=1
size_after_2=2
position_hello=0
position_world=1
word_at_0=hello
word_at_1=world

feature5_1_probabilistic_training_catego.description: Trains a probabilistic text classifier, returns accepted training tokens, and reports category state before and after adding a category.

training.text: love
Água

cases.expected_output:
stemmer=enabled
initial_categories=Interesting,Uninteresting
trained_Interesting=love
trained_Interesting=Água
final_categories=Interesting,Test,Uninteresting

feature5_2_dynamic_category_handling.jso.description: Handles training for categories that were not declared at creation time according to the configured dynamic-category policy.

training.text: A really sweet language

cases.expected_output:
categories=Interesting,Ruby,Uninteresting
category_count=3

training.text: A really sweet language

cases.expected_output:
error=category_not_found
category=Ruby

feature5_3_probabilistic_classification..description: Classifies text after supervised training and exposes both predicted category and rounded numeric confidence score.

training.text: here are some good words. I hope you love them
here are some bad words, I hate you

queries: I hate bad words and you

cases.expected_output:
query_0_category=Uninteresting
query_0_score=-4.85

feature5_4_threshold_classification.json.description: Applies an enabled confidence threshold so familiar inputs classify to the trained category and out-of-distribution inputs return no category.

cases.expected_output:
one=Number
two=Number
three=Number
four=Number
five=Number
xyzzy=none

feature5_5_untraining_changes_prediction.description: Removes previously learned evidence and reports that the prediction for an affected query changes after untraining.

training.text: here are some good words. I hope you love them
here are some bad words, I hate you
red orange green blue seven

cases.expected_output:
before=Colors
after=Uninteresting

feature5_6_custom_stopwords.json.description: Uses caller-supplied stopwords to skip all-stopword training text while allowing other text to train and affect classification scores.

training.text: Custom stopwords
To be or not to be

queries: To be or not to be

cases.expected_output:
categories=Stopwords
query_0_category=Stopwords
query_0_score=infinity
query_1_category=Stopwords
query_1_score=0.00

feature6_1_semantic_related_documents.js.description: Indexes documents semantically and returns the most related documents for a query in ranked order.

documents: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.
input.query: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.

cases.expected_output:
rank_0=This text involves dogs too. Dogs! 
rank_1=This text involves birds. Birds.
rank_2=This text revolves around cats. Cats.

feature6_2_semantic_classification_score.description: Assigns semantic categories to query documents and reports rounded scores for each classification.

training.text: This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.

queries: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text revolves around cats. Cats.

cases.expected_output:
query_0_category=Dog
query_0_score=2.49
query_1_category=Cat
query_1_score=1.41
query_2_category=Bird
query_2_score=2.00

feature6_3_semantic_scored_categories.js.description: Ranks semantic categories related to a mixed query and reports only categories with relevant evidence.

documents: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.

cases.expected_output:
result_count=2
rank_0=Bird:0.67
rank_1=Dog:0.69

feature6_4_semantic_context_classificati.description: Demonstrates that semantic classification can prefer contextual subject matter over a purely token-probability classifier on the same training set.

training.text: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.

cases.expected_output:
semantic_category=Dog
probabilistic_category=Cat

feature6_5_semantic_recategorization.jso.description: Allows categories attached to existing indexed documents to be changed without rebuilding the index, changing later classifications.

documents: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.

recategorize.text: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!

cases.expected_output:
before=Dog
needs_rebuild=no
after=Cow

feature6_6_semantic_search_ranking.json.description: Searches indexed documents by query text and returns ranked document matches for phrase and keyword queries.

documents: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.

cases.expected_output:
query_0=dog involves
query_0_rank_0=This text involves dogs too. Dogs! 
query_0_rank_1=This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
query_0_rank_2=This text also involves cats. Cats!
query_0_rank_3=This text involves birds. Birds.
query_0_rank_4=This text revolves around cats. Cats.
query_1=dog
query_1_rank_0=This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
query_1_rank_1=This text involves dogs too. Dogs! 
query_1_rank_2=This text also involves cats. Cats!
query_1_rank_3=This text involves birds. Birds.
query_1_rank_4=This text revolves around cats. Cats.

feature6_7_serialized_index_consistency..description: Serializes and restores a semantic index while preserving ranked search results.

documents: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.

cases.expected_output:
original_rank_0=This text revolves around cats. Cats.
original_rank_1=This text also involves cats. Cats!
original_rank_2=This text involves dogs too. Dogs! 
restored_rank_0=This text revolves around cats. Cats.
restored_rank_1=This text also involves cats. Cats!
restored_rank_2=This text involves dogs too. Dogs!

feature6_8_keywords_and_summary.json.description: Extracts highest-ranked keyword stems from a document and summarizes a document collection into a fixed number of representative sentences.

documents: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
This text involves dogs too. Dogs!
This text revolves around cats. Cats.
This text also involves cats. Cats!
This text involves birds. Birds.
input.keyword_source: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.
input.summary_source: This text deals with dogs. Dogs.This text involves dogs too. Dogs! This text revolves around cats. Cats.This text also involves cats. Cats!This text involves birds. Birds.

cases.expected_output:
keywords=dog,text,deal
summary=This text involves dogs too [...] This text also involves cats

feature6_9_document_validation_warning.j.description: Warns in normalized form when an added document contains only stopwords or words too short to produce usable terms.

cases.expected_output:
warning=empty_document
document=i can
